blocks:
Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ مَآ أَحَلَّ ٱللَّهُ لَكَ ۖ تَبْتَغِى مَرْضَاتَ أَزْوَٰجِكَ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١)
O Prophet! Why do you disallow [yourself] what Allah has made lawful for you, seeking to please your wives? And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (1)
قَدْ فَرَضَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ تَحِلَّةَ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢)
Allah has certainly made lawful for you the dissolution of your oaths, and Allah is your master and He is the All-knowing, the All-wise. (2)
وَإِذْ أَسَرَّ ٱلنَّبِىُّ إِلَىٰ بَعْضِ أَزْوَٰجِهِۦ حَدِيثًا فَلَمَّا نَبَّأَتْ بِهِۦ وَأَظْهَرَهُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ عَرَّفَ بَعْضَهُۥ وَأَعْرَضَ عَنۢ بَعْضٍ ۖ فَلَمَّا نَبَّأَهَا بِهِۦ قَالَتْ مَنْ أَنۢبَأَكَ هَـٰذَا ۖ قَالَ نَبَّأَنِىَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (٣)
When the Prophet confided a matter to one of his wives, but when she divulged it [instead of guarding the secret] and Allah apprised him about it, he acquainted [her] with part of the matter and ignored part of it. So when he told her about it, she said, ‘Who informed you about it?’ He said, ‘The All-knowing and the All-aware has informed me.’ (3)
إِن تَتُوبَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُمَا ۖ وَإِن تَظَـٰهَرَا عَلَيْهِ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ مَوْلَىٰهُ وَجِبْرِيلُ وَصَـٰلِحُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۖ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ ظَهِيرٌ (٤)
If the two of you repent to Allah... for your hearts have certainly swerved, and if you back each other against him, then [know that] Allah is indeed his guardian, and his supporters are Gabriel, the righteous among the faithful and, thereafter, the angels. (4)
عَسَىٰ رَبُّهُۥٓ إِن طَلَّقَكُنَّ أَن يُبْدِلَهُۥٓ أَزْوَٰجًا خَيْرًا مِّنكُنَّ مُسْلِمَـٰتٍ مُّؤْمِنَـٰتٍ قَـٰنِتَـٰتٍ تَـٰٓئِبَـٰتٍ عَـٰبِدَٰتٍ سَـٰٓئِحَـٰتٍ ثَيِّبَـٰتٍ وَأَبْكَارًا (٥)
It may be that if he divorces you his Lord will give him, in [your] stead, wives better than you: [such as are] muslim, faithful, obedient, penitent, devout and given to fasting, virgins and non-virgins. (5)

Content of Surah At-Tahrim

This Surah is mainly composed of four sections:

The first section: which continues from verse 1 to 5, relates to the incident of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله with some of his wives. The Prophet prohibited some lawful foods for himself, and the above verses were revealed, reproaching the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله wives; the details of which will come in the "Occasion of Revelation," God willing.

The second section: which continues from verse 6 to 8, is a general address to all believers regarding care in the education and upbringing of the family, and the necessity of repentance from sins.

The third section: which consists of only one verse, is addressed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding Jihad against the disbelievers and the hypocrites.

In the fourth section: which forms the final part of the Surah and includes verses 10 to 12, Allah, in order to clarify the previous sections, describes the lives of two righteous women (Mary and the wife of Pharaoh) and two unrighteous women (the wife of Noah and the wife of Lot). In fact, He warns Muslim women, and especially the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, to align themselves with the first group and not the second.

Virtue of Reciting the Surah

In a Hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated: "Whoever recites this Surah, اعطاه اللَّه توبة نصوحا : Allah will grant him the success of sincere repentance." [1] And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever recites Surah al-Talaq and at-Tahrim in an obligatory prayer, Allah will protect him on the Day of Resurrection from fear and sorrow, and will deliver him from the fire of Hell, and will admit him into Paradise because of his recitation of these two Surahs and his persistence in it, for these two Surahs belong to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله." 2

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 311. 2: "Thawab al-A'mal," as quoted in Tafsir "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 367.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ ما أَحَلَّ اللَّهُ لَكَ تَبْتَغِي مَرْضاتَ أَزْواجِكَ وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ (1) قَدْ فَرَضَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ تَحِلَّةَ أَيْمانِكُمْ وَ اللَّهُ مَوْلاكُمْ وَ هُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ (2) وَ إِذْ أَسَرَّ النَّبِيُّ إِلى‌ بَعْضِ أَزْواجِهِ حَدِيثاً فَلَمَّا نَبَّأَتْ بِهِ وَ أَظْهَرَهُ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ عَرَّفَ بَعْضَهُ وَ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ بَعْضٍ فَلَمَّا نَبَّأَها بِهِ قالَتْ مَنْ أَنْبَأَكَ هذا قالَ نَبَّأَنِيَ الْعَلِيمُ الْخَبِيرُ (3) إِنْ تَتُوبا إِلَى اللَّهِ فَقَدْ صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُما وَ إِنْ تَظاهَرا عَلَيْهِ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ مَوْلاهُ وَ جِبْرِيلُ وَ صالِحُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ ظَهِيرٌ (4) عَسى‌ رَبُّهُ إِنْ طَلَّقَكُنَّ أَنْ يُبْدِلَهُ أَزْواجاً خَيْراً مِنْكُنَّ مُسْلِماتٍ مُؤْمِناتٍ قانِتاتٍ تائِباتٍ عابِداتٍ سائِحاتٍ ثَيِّباتٍ وَ أَبْكاراً (5)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation of the above verses, many narrations have been cited in the books of commentary, Hadith, and history of both the Shi'a and Sunni, from which we have selected what seems most famous and appropriate, which is as follows:

Whenever the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله visited "Zaynab bint Jahsh" (one of his wives), Zaynab would keep him and serve him honey she had prepared. This news reached "Aisha" and it weighed heavily upon her. She says: I and "Hafsa" (another wife of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله) agreed that whenever the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله came to either of us, we would immediately say: "Have you eaten 'Maghafir' resin?!" ("Maghafir" was a resin secreted by a tree in the Hijaz called "Urfut" which had an unpleasant odor). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was committed to never having an unpleasant smell from his mouth or clothes; on the contrary, he insisted on always being fragrant and perfumed! Thus, one day the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله came to "Hafsa," and she said this to him. The Prophet said: "I have not eaten Maghafir, but rather I drank honey at Zaynab bint Jahsh's place, and I swear that I will never drink that honey again (lest the bee of that honey had sat on an unpleasant plant, possibly Maghafir), but do not tell this to anyone" (lest it reach the people's ears and they ask why the Prophet prohibited a lawful food for himself, or they follow the Prophet's action in this or similar cases, or it reaches Zaynab and she becomes heartbroken).

However, she eventually disclosed this secret, and it later became clear that the root of this matter was a plot. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله became deeply upset, and the above verses were revealed, ending the matter in such a way that such actions would not be repeated within the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله household. [1] In some narrations, it is also mentioned that after this incident, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله withdrew from his wives for one month 2, and even rumors of his decision to divorce them spread, such that they became severely terrified [3] and regretted their actions.

Commentary

Severe Reproach Toward Some of the Prophet's (ص) Wives

Undoubtedly, a great man like the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله does not belong only to himself, but to the entire Islamic society and the world of humanity. Therefore, if plots are carried out against him inside his home, even if seemingly small and insignificant, they should not be passed over lightly. His dignity must not—God forbid—become a plaything for anyone, and if such a plan arises, it must be dealt with decisively.

The above verses are, in fact, a manifestation of decisiveness from Allah the Almighty in the face of such an incident, and for the preservation of His Prophet's dignity.

First, the address is directed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself, saying: "O Prophet! Why do you prohibit what Allah has made lawful for you, seeking to please your wives?" (يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ ما أَحَلَّ اللَّهُ لَكَ تَبْتَغِي مَرْضاتَ أَزْواجِكَ).

It is clear that this prohibition was not a legislative (Shar'i) prohibition, but rather, as understood from the subsequent verses, it was an oath taken by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and we know that swearing an oath to abstain from some permissible things is not a sin.

Therefore, the phrase "لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ" (Why do you prohibit?) is not by way of rebuke or reproach, but rather a type of sympathy and compassion.

It is exactly like when we say to someone who works hard to earn an income but does not benefit much from it himself: "Why do you trouble yourself so much and not benefit from the result of this labor?"

Then, at the end of the verse, He adds: "And Allah is Forgiving, Merciful" (وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

This forgiveness and mercy are toward the wives who brought about the causes of that incident; if they truly repent, they will be included in it. Or it is an indication that it was better for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله not to have taken such an oath—an act that potentially emboldened some of his wives صلى الله عليه وآله.

In the next verse, He adds: "Allah has already ordained for you the dissolution of your oaths (in such cases)" (قَدْ فَرَضَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ تَحِلَّةَ أَيْمانِكُمْ) 1 in such a way that you pay the expiation (kaffarah) for the oath and free yourselves.

Of course, if an oath is in a case where performing the act is preferable, one must fulfill the oath, and breaking it is a sin and requires expiation. But if it is in cases where abstaining from that act is less desirable (like the verse under discussion), then breaking it is permissible, but to maintain respect for the oath, it is better that expiation also be given. [3] Then He adds: "And Allah is your Protector, and He is the All-Knowing, the All-Wise" (وَ اللَّهُ مَوْلاكُمْ وَ هُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ).

Therefore, He has smoothed the way for you to be delivered from such oaths, and according to His knowledge and wisdom, He has resolved the problem for you.

It is understood from narrations that after the revelation of this verse, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله freed a slave and made lawful what he had prohibited for himself through the oath.

[1]: Raghib says in "Al-Mufradat": Whenever "Farada" is accompanied by "Ala," it means "obligation," and wherever it is with "Lam," it means "absence of prohibition." Therefore, in the verse under discussion, "Farada" does not mean obligation, but rather permission.

[3]: The expiation for an oath, as understood from verse 89 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, consists of feeding ten needy people, or clothing ten people, or freeing a slave; and whoever cannot afford any of these shall fast for three days (refer to Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 61).

In the next verse, He provides more detail regarding this incident, saying: "And remember when the Prophet confided a matter to one of his wives, but when she disclosed it and Allah made it known to him, he made known part of it and avoided part of it" (وَ إِذْ أَسرَّ النَّبِيُّ إِلى‌ بَعْضِ أَزْواجِهِ حَدِيثاً فَلَمَّا نَبَّأَتْ بِهِ وَ أَظْهَرَهُ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ عَرَّفَ بَعْضَهُ وَ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ بَعْضٍ).

What was this secret that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله told one of his wives, which she did not keep? According to what we said in the occasion of revelation, this secret consisted of two matters: one was the drinking of honey at the home of his wife Zaynab bint Jahsh, and the other was his prohibition of drinking it for himself in the future. The non-confidential wife intended in this verse was "Hafsa," who heard this and recounted it to "Aisha."

Since the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had become aware of this disclosure of the secret through revelation, he mentioned part of it to "Hafsa," and so that she would not be overly ashamed and embarrassed, he refrained from mentioning the other part (it is possible the first part was the act of drinking honey and the second part was its prohibition upon himself).

In any case, "When the Prophet informed her (Hafsa) of this disclosure of the secret, she said: 'Who informed you of this?'" (فَلَمَّا نَبَّأَها بِهِ قالَتْ مَنْ أَنْبَأَكَ هذا).

"He said: 'The All-Knowing, the All-Aware informed me'" (قالَ نَبَّأَنِيَ الْعَلِيمُ الْخَبِيرُ).

From the entirety of this verse, it appears that some of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله wives not only upset him with their words, but also the matter of keeping secrets—which is among the most important conditions of a loyal spouse—was not present in them. Yet, conversely, the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله behavior toward them, despite all these qualities, was so noble that he was not even willing to confront her with the entire secret she had disclosed; he only alluded to a part of it. Hence, in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام, it is said: ما استقصى كريم قط، لان اللَّه يقول عَرَّفَ بَعْضَهُ وَ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ بَعْضٍ : "Noble and generous people never go to the final stage in claiming their personal rights, for Allah says here regarding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: He informed of a part and refrained from a part." [1]

Then He addresses these two wives who had a hand in the above plot, saying: "If you two repent to Allah, [it is better for you], for your hearts have certainly deviated [from the truth] and become tainted with sin" (إِنْ تَتُوبا إِلَى اللَّهِ فَقَدْ صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُما). By consensus of Shi'a and Sunni commentators, these two individuals are "Hafsa" and "Aisha," who were respectively the daughters of "Umar" and "Abu Bakr."

"Saghat" is from the root "Saghw" (on the weight of Afw), meaning to incline toward something. Thus they say: "Saghat al-Nujum," meaning the stars inclined toward the west. For this reason, the word "Isgha'" has come to mean listening to the speech of another, and the meaning of "صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُما" in the verse under discussion is the deviation of their hearts from the truth toward sin. 2

[1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 19, p. 392. 2: According to the interpretation mentioned above, which many commentators have chosen, the verse has an omitted part (Mahdhuf), and the estimation is: "If you two repent to Allah, it would be better for you" (or another estimation similar to this meaning). However, some have suggested that there is no omitted part and the phrase "صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُما" is the recompense (Jaza') of the condition (with the proviso that the concept of the phrase be inclination toward the truth, not falsehood). But this possibility is very remote, because the condition is in the form of a present tense verb and the recompense is in the form of a past tense verb, and this is not permissible according to most grammarians. Additionally, the mention of "Qulubakuma" (your hearts) in the plural form, rather than the dual form, is because two duals next to each other are unpleasant in terms of eloquence, so it was mentioned in the plural form while its meaning is dual.

Then He adds: "But if you two cooperate against him, you will achieve nothing, for Allah is his Protector and Helper, and so are Gabriel and the righteous believers, and the angels, moreover, are his assistants" (وَ إِنْ تَظاهَرا عَلَيْهِ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ مَوْلاهُ وَ جِبْرِيلُ وَ صالِحُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ ظَهِيرٌ).

This expression shows to what extent this incident had a negative impact on the pure heart of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his great soul, to the point that Allah rose to defend him, and although His own power is sufficient in every respect, He also announces the support of Gabriel, the righteous believers, and the other angels.

It is noteworthy that: In "Sahih Bukhari," it is narrated from "Ibn Abbas" who says: I asked Umar, "Who were those two wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله who had cooperated against him?" Umar said: "They were Hafsa and Aisha." Then he added: "By Allah, in the era of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), we did not consider women to be anything until Allah revealed verses regarding them and assigned rights for them (and they became bold)." [1] In the commentary "Durr al-Manthur," this same meaning is narrated within a detailed Hadith from Ibn Abbas,

[1]: "Sahih Bukhari," Vol. 6, p. 195 (under Surah At-Tahrim).

and in that Hadith, it is mentioned that Umar says: "After this incident, I learned that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had withdrawn from all his wives and stayed in a place called 'Mashrabat Umm Ibrahim.' I came to him and said: 'O Messenger of Allah! Have you divorced your wives?' He said: 'No.' I said: 'Allahu Akbar! We, the people of Quraysh, were always dominant over our women, but when we came to Medina, we saw a group whose women were dominant over them; our women also learned from them! One day I saw my wife arguing with me; I found this action of hers strange and ugly. She said: Why are you surprised? By Allah, the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله do the same with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله! They even sometimes shun him.' And I advised my daughter Hafsa never to do such a thing and said: 'If your neighbor (meaning Aisha) does this, do not do it, for her circumstances are different from yours.'" [1] Regarding "Salih al-Mu'minin" (the righteous of the believers), we have a discussion that will come in the "Key Points," God willing.

In the last verse under discussion, Allah addresses all the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله with a tone not devoid of threat, saying: "Perhaps his Lord, if he divorces you, will give him in your stead wives better than you—submitting [to Allah], believing, devoutly obedient, repentant, worshipping, and fasting—previously married and virgins" (عَسى‌ رَبُّهُ إِنْ طَلَّقَكُنَّ أَنْ يُبْدِلَهُ أَزْواجاً خَيْراً مِنْكُنَّ مُسْلِماتٍ مُؤْمِناتٍ قانِتاتٍ تائِباتٍ عابِداتٍ سائِحاتٍ ثَيِّباتٍ وَ أَبْكاراً). In this way, He warns them not to imagine that the Prophet will never

[1]: "Durr al-Manthur," Vol. 6, p. 243 (summarized).

divorce them, and also not to imagine that if he divorces them, wives better than them will not replace them. They should stop plotting, arguing, and causing distress; otherwise, they will be forever deprived of the honor of being the Prophet's wives, and women better and more virtuous will take their place!

Key Points

1- Attributes of a Worthy Spouse

Here, the Quran lists six attributes for good wives that can serve as a model for all Muslims when choosing a spouse.

First "Islam" and then "Iman" (faith), meaning a belief that penetrates the depths of the human heart; then the state of "Qunut," meaning humility and obedience to the husband; after that "Tawbah" (repentance), meaning if a wrong act is committed by her, she does not persist in her mistake and enters through the door of apology. After that "Ibadah" (worship) of Allah—a worship that builds her soul and spirit and makes it pure and clean; and then "obedience to Allah's command" and avoidance of every kind of sin.

It is noteworthy that many commentators have interpreted "Saihat" (plural of Saih) to mean "Sa'im" (fasting), but as Raghib says in "Al-Mufradat," fasting is of two kinds: "real fasting," which means abstaining from food and intercourse, and "judgmental fasting," which means guarding the limbs of the body from sins, and the meaning of fasting here is the second meaning (this statement of Raghib seems interesting given the context, but it should be known that "Saih" has also been interpreted as one who travels in the

way of obedience to Allah). [1] It is also noteworthy that the Quran did not emphasize whether the woman is a virgin or non-virgin, and did not attach much importance to it, because in the face of the spiritual attributes mentioned, this matter does not have much importance.

2- Who is meant by "صالِحُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ"?

Undoubtedly, "صالِحُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ" has a broad meaning that includes all righteous, God-fearing believers with complete faith, even though "Salih" here is singular and not plural, but because it has a generic meaning, universality is understood from it. 2 But as for who is the most perfect and complete instance of it here? It is understood from numerous narrations that it refers to the "Commander of the Faithful" Ali عليه السلام.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: لقد عرف رسول اللَّه (ص) عليا (ع) اصحابه مرتين: اما مرة فحيث قال: "من كنت مولاه فعلى مولاه" و اما الثانية فحيث نزلت هذه الاية: "فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ مَوْلاهُ وَ جِبْرِيلُ وَ صالِحُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ ..." اخذ رسول اللَّه (ص) بيد على (ع) فقال: ايها الناس هذا صالح المؤمنين!! "The Messenger of Allah introduced Ali عليه السلام (explicitly) to his companions twice: once when he said (at Ghadir Khum): 'Whomever I am his master, Ali is his master,' and the second time when the verse 'فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ مَوْلاهُ ...' was revealed, the Messenger of Allah took Ali's عليه السلام hand and said: 'O people, this is the Righteous of the Believers!'" [1] Many Sunni scholars have also narrated this meaning in their books, including Allamah Tha'labi, Allamah Ganji in "Kifayat al-Talib," Abu Hayyan al-Andalusi, Sibt ibn al-Jawzi, and others. 2 A group of commentators, including al-Suyuti in "Durr al-Manthur" under the verse in question, al-Qurtubi in his famous commentary, and also al-Alusi in "Ruh al-Ma'ani" in the commentary of this same verse, have narrated this tradition.

The author of "Ruh al-Bayan," after quoting this narration from Mujahid, says: "Supporting this Hadith is the famous Hadith of 'Manzilah' where the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to Ali عليه السلام: 'You are to me as Aaron was to Moses,' because the title 'Salihin' (the righteous) has appeared in Quranic verses for the Prophets, including in the verse 'وَ كُلًّا جَعَلْنا صالِحِينَ' (Al-Anbiya-72) and in the verse 'أَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ' (Yusuf-101) (where in the first, the title 'Salih' is applied to a group of Prophets, and in the second to Prophet Joseph); and when Ali عليه السلام is in the position of 'Aaron,' he too is an instance of 'Salih' (reflect on this)."

In short: the Hadiths in this regard are numerous. The famous commentator and traditionist Bahrani, in Tafsir "Al-Burhan," after mentioning a narration in this regard from Muhammad ibn

[1]: "Saih" from the root "Siyahat" (traveling) originally referred to world travelers who set out without provisions and lived on the help of people; and since the fasting person abstains from food until the time of Iftar arrives, and in this respect is similar to travelers, this word was applied to the "Sa'im" (fasting person). 2: Some have considered "Salih" here to be plural and said that since the 'Waw' of "Salihu" is omitted when in a construct state (Idhafah), it also did not appear in the Quranic script, but this meaning seems remote. [1]: Tafsir "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 316. 2: For further explanation, refer to Vol. 3 of "Ihqaq al-Haq," p. 311.

Abbas [1], mentions that he collected 52 Hadiths in this regard from both Shi'a and Sunni sources, then he himself proceeds to quote a part of them. 2

3- The Prophet's (ص) Dissatisfaction with Some of His Wives

Throughout history, there are many great men who did not have wives worthy of them and suffered from the lack of necessary qualities in them, and in the Holy Quran, examples of them among the great Prophets are mentioned.

The above verses show that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was like this toward some of his wives; due to the rivalries they had with each other, they sometimes wounded his pure soul, and sometimes they would protest against him or disclose his secret, to the point that Allah rose to reproach them and defend His Prophet, and uttered the most emphatic statement in this regard, even threatening them with divorce. And as we saw, after the incident of the above verses, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله shunned his wives for about a month, perhaps they would reform themselves! In principle, the history of his life clearly shows that some of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله wives not only lacked the necessary recognition of the status of Prophethood, but sometimes they would question him like an ordinary person, and even—God forbid—insult him! Therefore, the insistence on the idea that all of them were worthy

[1]: It seems that "Muhammad ibn Abbas" here is the same "Abu Abdillah" known as "Ibn al-Hajjam," the author of the book "Ma Nazala min al-Quran fi Ahl al-Bayt" (ع), of which a group of scholars have said: it is a book the like of which has not been authored until now (Jami' al-Ruwat, Vol. 2, p. 134). 2: Tafsir "Al-Burhan," Vol. 4, p. 353, under Hadith 2.

and perfect individuals seems groundless, especially given the explicitness of the above verses.

The history of Islam regarding the women after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, especially the story of the Battle of "Jamal," also showed that this meaning was repeated not only in his era, but after him toward his successors as well, though this is not the place to detail all these issues.

In principle, the expression of the above verses which says: "If the Prophet divorces you, Allah will give him wives better than you who possess the six attributes mentioned in the verse" reflects the reality that at least some of the Prophet's wives did not possess these conditions.

Referring to the verses of Surah Al-Ahzab regarding the Prophet's wives also confirms the above view.

4- Disclosure of Secrets

Keeping secrets is not only among the attributes of true believers, but every person of character must be a keeper of secrets. This meaning holds greater importance regarding close friends and a spouse. In the above verses, we read how Allah severely blames and reproaches some of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله wives for failing to keep a secret.

Ali عليه السلام says in a Hadith: جمع خير الدنيا و الآخره فى كتمان السر و مصادقة الاخيار، و جمع الشر فى الاذاعة و مواخاة الاشرار "All the good of this world and the Hereafter is hidden in these two things: keeping secrets and friendship with the good; and all evil is hidden in these two things: disclosing secrets and friendship with the wicked." [1]

[1]: "Safinat al-Bihar," Vol. 2, p. 469 (under the root Katm).

5- You Must Not Prohibit Allah's Lawful Things for Yourself

Matters that have been made lawful or prohibited by Allah were all according to precise wisdoms, and for this reason, there is no place for a human to prohibit a lawful thing for himself or make a prohibited thing lawful for himself. Even if one takes an oath in this regard—as mentioned in the above verses—that oath can be broken.

Yes, if a permissible thing that one swore to abstain from was a disliked (Makruh) act, or if from some aspect its abandonment was better, in that case, one must adhere to the oath.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ قُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَأَهْلِيكُمْ نَارًا وَقُودُهَا ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱلْحِجَارَةُ عَلَيْهَا مَلَـٰٓئِكَةٌ غِلَاظٌ شِدَادٌ لَّا يَعْصُونَ ٱللَّهَ مَآ أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ مَا يُؤْمَرُونَ (٦)
O you who have faith! Save yourselves and your families from a Fire whose fuel will be people and stones, over which are [assigned] severe and mighty angels, who do not disobey whatever Allah commands them and carry out what they are commanded. (6)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَا تَعْتَذِرُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۖ إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٧)
[They will call out to the faithless:] ‘O faithless ones! Do not make any excuses today. You are being requited only for what you used to do.’ (7)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوْبَةً نَّصُوحًا عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يُكَفِّرَ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَيُدْخِلَكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ يَوْمَ لَا يُخْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّبِىَّ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ ۖ نُورُهُمْ يَسْعَىٰ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ أَتْمِمْ لَنَا نُورَنَا وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٨)
O you who have faith! Repent to Allah with sincere repentance! Maybe your Lord will absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you into gardens with streams running in them, on the day when Allah will not let down the Prophet and the faithful who are with him. Their light will move swiftly before them and on their right. They will say, ‘Our Lord! Perfect our light for us, and forgive us! Indeed You have power over all things.’ (8)

Commentary

Save Your Family from the Fire of Hell!

Following the warning and reproach directed at some of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives, Allah addresses all believers in the verses under discussion, providing instructions regarding the education and upbringing of spouses, children, and the family. First, He says:

"O you who have believed, protect yourselves and your families from a Fire whose fuel is people and stones" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا قُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ وَأَهْلِيكُمْ نَاراً وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ وَالْحِجَارَةُ).

Protecting oneself is achieved by abandoning sins and not surrendering to rebellious desires. Protecting one's family is achieved through education, upbringing, enjoining what is good, forbidding what is evil, and providing a pure environment free from any pollution within the atmosphere of the home and family.

This is a program that must begin from the very first foundation stone of the family—namely, the preliminaries of marriage—and then from the first moment of a child's birth, and it must be pursued with correct planning and utmost precision through all stages.

In other words, the rights of a wife and children are not fulfilled merely by providing for their living expenses, housing, and nutrition; more important than that is nourishing their souls and spirits and employing the principles of correct education and upbringing.

It is noteworthy that the expression "قُوا" (protect/ward off) indicates that if you leave them to themselves, they will inevitably move toward the Fire of Hell; it is you who must preserve them from falling into the Fire.

"وقود" (waqud), as we have previously mentioned, means "fuel"—that is, flammable material like "firewood" (not "kindling" or a "striker" like a match, which the Arabs call "zinad").

Thus, the Fire of Hell is not like the fires of this world; its flames flare up from within the very existence of humans! And from within stones! Not just sulfurous stones, as some commentators have suggested, but all types of stones, as the wording of the verse is absolute. Today we know that every piece of stone is composed of billions upon billions of atoms; if the energy stored within them were released, it would create such a fire that it would dazzle mankind.

Some commentators have interpreted "حجارة" (stones) here as the idols that were made of stone and worshipped by the polytheists.

Then He adds: "Over it are angels, harsh and severe; they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them but do what they are commanded" (عَلَيْهَا مَلَائِكَةٌ غِلَاظٌ شِدَادٌ لَا يَعْصُونَ اللَّهَ مَا أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ مَا يُؤْمَرُونَ).

Thus, there is no way to escape, nor will weeping, pleading, or lamentation be effective.

It is clear that any official appointed for a task must have a temperament suited to it; the officials of punishment must naturally be harsh, for Hell is not a center of mercy, but a center of Divine wrath and anger. Yet, at the same time, these officials never exceed the bounds of justice and execute Allah's command without the slightest deficiency.

A group of commentators has raised a question here: how is the expression regarding the lack of disobedience in the above verse compatible with the issue of the absence of religious obligation (taklif) on the Day of Resurrection?

However, it must be noted that the obedience and lack of disobedience of the angels is a type of "existential obedience" (takwini), not "legislative obedience" (tashri'i). Existential obedience always exists; in other words, they are created in such a way that they execute Divine commands with full inclination, desire, and love, while still possessing volition.

In the next verse, He addresses the disbelievers and describes their state on that day, saying: "O you who have disbelieved, make no excuses today. You are only being recompensed for what you used to do" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَا تَعْتَذِرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ).

The placement of this verse after the previous verse—which addressed the believers—is an allusion to the reality that if you do not attend to the state of your wife, children, and family, your situation may reach a point where you become the addressee of this speech on the Day of Resurrection.

The expression "إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ" (You are only being recompensed for what you used to do) once again confirms the truth that the recompense of sinners in the Hereafter is their very deeds, which will appear before them and remain with them. The expression in the previous verse that the Fire of Hell flares up from within the existence of humans also supports this meaning.

It is also noteworthy that the refusal to accept excuses there is because making an excuse is a form of repentance, and repentance is only possible in this world, not in the other world, nor after entering Hell.

The next verse actually shows the way to salvation from the Fire of Hell, saying: "O you who have believed, turn to Allah in repentance, a sincere repentance" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا تُوبُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ تَوْبَةً نَصُوحاً).

Yes, the first step toward salvation is repentance from sin—a repentance that is sincere in every respect; a repentance motivated by Allah's command and the fear of sin, not by dread of its social or worldly consequences; a repentance that separates man from disobedience forever, with no return to it occurring.

We know that the reality of "repentance" (tawbah) is remorse and regret for the sin, which necessitates a resolve to abandon it in the future; and if it was an act that can be compensated for, one should seek to compensate for it. Uttering "istighfar" (seeking forgiveness) also expresses this same meaning. Thus, the pillars of repentance can be summarized in five things: "abandoning the sin, remorse, resolve to abandon it in the future, compensating for the past, and seeking forgiveness."

"Nasuh" is derived from the root "nas-h" (نصح), which originally means sincere well-wishing; hence, pure honey is called "nasih." Since true well-wishing must be accompanied by firm work, the word "nas-h" is sometimes used in this sense as well. For this reason, a firm stitch is called "nisah" and a tailor is called "nasih." Both these meanings—namely "being pure" and "being firm"—must be present in "nasuh" repentance [1]. Regarding what "nasuh repentance" is, many interpretations have been mentioned, to the extent that some have counted up to twenty-three interpretations [2]. However, almost all these interpretations return to one reality, or they are various branches and conditions of repentance.

Among them is that "nasuh repentance" is that which possesses four conditions: Heartfelt remorse, verbal seeking of forgiveness, abandoning the sin, and resolve to abandon it in the future.

Others have said: "nasuh repentance" is that which possesses three conditions: Fear that it might not be accepted, hope that it will be accepted, and continued obedience to Allah.

Or that "nasuh repentance" is that you always see your sin before your eyes and feel ashamed of it! Or that "nasuh repentance" is to return usurped rights to their owners, seek pardon from the oppressed, and persist in obedience to Allah.

[1]: Some imagine that "Nasuh" was the name of a specific person and have narrated a detailed story in this regard as the "Repentance of Nasuh," but it should be noted that Nasuh is not a person's name; rather, it has a descriptive meaning, although the famous story might be true.

[2]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 6766.

Or that "nasuh repentance" is that which contains three conditions: speaking little, eating little, and sleeping little.

Or that "nasuh repentance" is that which is accompanied by a weeping eye and a heart that loathes sin... and similar descriptions, all of which are branches of one reality: a sincere and complete repentance.

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read that when Mu'adh ibn Jabal asked about "nasuh repentance," he replied: "It is that the repenter repents and then never returns to the sin, just as milk never returns to the udder!" [1] This elegant expression describes the reality that nasuh repentance creates such a revolution in a person that it completely closes the way of returning to the past, just as it is impossible for milk to return to the udder.

This meaning, which has also appeared in other narrations, actually describes the highest degree of nasuh repentance; otherwise, in lower stages, there might be a return which, after repeated repentance, eventually leads to permanent abandonment.

Then, referring to the effects of this nasuh repentance, He adds: "It may be that your Lord will remit from you your misdeeds" (عَسَى رَبُّكُمْ أَنْ يُكَفِّرَ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئَاتِكُمْ).

"And admit you into gardens beneath which rivers flow" (وَيُدْخِلَكُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ).

"This will be on the Day when Allah will not disgrace the Prophet and those who believed with him" (يَوْمَ لَا يُخْزِي اللَّهُ النَّبِيَّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ).

"While their light (of faith and righteous deeds) proceeds before them and on their right," illuminating the arena of the Gathering and opening their way toward Paradise (نُورُهُمْ يَسْعَى بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمَانِهِمْ).

It is here that they turn to the threshold of Allah and "say: 'Our Lord, perfect for us our light and forgive us. Indeed, You are over all things competent'" (يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا أَتْمِمْ لَنَا نُورَنَا وَاغْفِرْ لَنَا إِنَّكَ عَلَى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

In reality, this nasuh repentance has five great fruits: First: Forgiveness of misdeeds and sins. Second: Entry into the Paradise of Divine blessings. Third: Absence of disgrace on that day when the veils are removed, realities are revealed, and the criminal liars are humiliated and disgraced. Yes, on that day, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the believers will be honorable, for what they said will manifest as reality. Fourth: That the light of their faith and deeds moves before them and to their right, illuminating their path toward Paradise (some commentators have considered the light moving in front to be the light of "deeds"; we had another interpretation regarding this under verse 12 of Surah Al-Hadid in Volume 23 of Tafsir Nemouneh). Fifth: That their attention toward Allah increases; hence, they turn toward Allah's threshold and request from Him the completion of their light and the full forgiveness of their sins.

Key Points

1- Education and Upbringing of the Family

The command to enjoin what is good and forbid what is evil is a general command that all Muslims have toward one another. However, from the above verses and the narrations regarding the rights of children and the like found in Islamic sources, it is clearly understood that a person has a heavier responsibility toward his spouse and children. He is duty-bound to strive as much as he can in their education and upbringing, to restrain them from sin, and to invite them to good deeds, rather than sufficing only with nourishing their bodies.

In reality, the great society is formed from small units called "families," and whenever these small units—which are easier to manage—are reformed, the entire society is reformed. This responsibility lies primarily on the shoulders of fathers and mothers.

Especially in our era, when the crushing waves of corruption outside families are very strong and dangerous, a more fundamental and precise planning must be carried out to neutralize them through family education and upbringing.

Not only the fires of the Hereafter, but also the fires of this world originate from within the existence of humans, and everyone is responsible for protecting their family from these fires.

In a hadith, we read: "When the above verse was revealed, one of the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) asked: 'How can I protect my family from the Fire of Hell?' The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) replied: 'You command them to what Allah has commanded, and you forbid them from what Allah has forbidden. If they obey you, you have protected them, and if they disobey you, you have fulfilled your duty.'" [1]

In another comprehensive and beautiful hadith from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), it is stated: "Behold, each of you is a guardian, and each of you is responsible for his subjects. The leader over the people is a guardian and is responsible for them; a man is a guardian over the members of his household and is responsible for them; a woman is a guardian over her husband's household and his children and is responsible for them. Behold, each of you is a guardian, and each of you is responsible for his subjects!" [2]

We conclude this extensive discussion with a hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام). The Imam (عليه السلام) said in the interpretation of the above verse: "Teach yourselves and your families good, and discipline them." [3]

2- Repentance: A Door to Allah's Mercy

It often happens that a person—especially at the beginning of upbringing and the journey toward Allah—commits slips. If the doors of return were closed to him, he would become despondent and remain stalled forever. Therefore, in the educational school of Islam, repentance is presented as an educational principle of extraordinary importance, inviting all sinners to enter through this door to reform themselves and compensate for the past.

Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام) in the "Supplication of the Repenters" (Munajat al-Ta'ibin) addresses Allah's threshold: "My God, You are the One who opened for Your servants a door to Your pardon and named it 'repentance,' saying: 'Turn to Allah in sincere repentance.' What then is the excuse of one who neglects entering the door after its opening?" [1]

The issue of repentance has been emphasized so much in narrations that we read in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام): "Indeed, Allah the Almighty is more joyful at the repentance of His servant than a man who loses his mount and his provisions in a dark night and then finds them!" [2] These expressions, filled with magnanimity, are all to encourage this vital matter.

However, it must be noted that "repentance" is not merely a verbal utterance or saying "I seek forgiveness from Allah" (astaghfirullah); rather, it has conditions and pillars that were mentioned in the interpretation of "nasuh repentance" in the verses above. Whenever repentance is performed with these conditions, it leaves such an effect that it completely erases the sin and its traces from the soul and spirit of man. Thus, we read in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام): "The one who repents from sin is like one who has no sin, and the one who persists in sin while seeking forgiveness from it is like one who mocks!" [1]

We have had other detailed discussions regarding repentance (in Volume 3, page 312 onwards) under verse 17 of Surah An-Nisa and (Volume 19, page 498 onwards) under verse 53 of Az-Zumar.

[1]: Mجمع al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 318. [2]: Majmu'at Warram, Vol. 1, p. 6. [3]: Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 244. [1]: The Fifteen Whispered Prayers, First Prayer (Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 94, p. 142). [2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Repentance, Hadith 8. [1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Repentance, Hadith 10.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ جَـٰهِدِ ٱلْكُفَّارَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٩)
O Prophet! Wage jihad against the faithless and the hypocrites, and be severe with them. Their refuge will be hell, and it is an evil destination. (9)
ضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱمْرَأَتَ نُوحٍ وَٱمْرَأَتَ لُوطٍ ۖ كَانَتَا تَحْتَ عَبْدَيْنِ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا صَـٰلِحَيْنِ فَخَانَتَاهُمَا فَلَمْ يُغْنِيَا عَنْهُمَا مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا وَقِيلَ ٱدْخُلَا ٱلنَّارَ مَعَ ٱلدَّٰخِلِينَ (١٠)
Allah cites an example of the faithless: the wife of Noah and the wife of Lot. They were under two of our righteous servants, yet they betrayed them. So they did not avail them in any way against Allah, and it was said [to them], ‘Enter the Fire, along with those who enter [it].’ (10)
وَضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱمْرَأَتَ فِرْعَوْنَ إِذْ قَالَتْ رَبِّ ٱبْنِ لِى عِندَكَ بَيْتًا فِى ٱلْجَنَّةِ وَنَجِّنِى مِن فِرْعَوْنَ وَعَمَلِهِۦ وَنَجِّنِى مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١١)
Allah cites an example of the faithful: the wife of Pharaoh, when she said, ‘My Lord! Build me a home near You in paradise, and deliver me from Pharaoh and his conduct, and deliver me from the wrongdoing lot.’ (11)
وَمَرْيَمَ ٱبْنَتَ عِمْرَٰنَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَهَا فَنَفَخْنَا فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِنَا وَصَدَّقَتْ بِكَلِمَـٰتِ رَبِّهَا وَكُتُبِهِۦ وَكَانَتْ مِنَ ٱلْقَـٰنِتِينَ (١٢)
And Mary, daughter of Imran, who guarded the chastity of her womb, so We breathed into it of Our spirit. She confirmed the words of her Lord and His Books, and she was one of the obedient. (12)

Commentary

Models of Believing and Disbelieving Women

Since the hypocrites were certainly pleased by the disclosure of the internal secrets of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) house and the emergence of disputes and differences among his wives—as mentioned in the previous verses—and were likely fueling rumors in this regard, perhaps for this reason, the first verse under discussion commands firm action against them, stating:

"O Prophet! Strive against the disbelievers and the hypocrites and be harsh upon them. Their abode is Hell, and wretched is the destination."

(يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ جَاهِدِ الْكُفَّارَ وَالْمُنَافِقِينَ وَاغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَمَأْوَاهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ).

This jihad regarding the disbelievers may have been in the form of armed or unarmed struggle, but regarding the hypocrites, it was undoubtedly not armed jihad. This is because it is nowhere recorded in history that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) engaged in armed combat against the hypocrites. Therefore, we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) who said:

"The Messenger of Allah never fought a hypocrite; rather, he always sought to win over their hearts." [^1]

Thus, the meaning of jihad against them is rebuking, censuring, threatening, warning, and exposing them, or in some cases, winning over their hearts. This is because "jihad" has a broad and expansive meaning, encompassing every kind of effort and struggle. The expression "وَ اغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ" (and be harsh upon them) also refers to severity in speech, exposure, threats, and the like.

This specific stance toward the hypocrites, despite them being the most dangerous enemies of Islam, was because they outwardly claimed to be Muslims and mingled fully with the believers; hence, it was not possible to treat them exactly like a disbeliever.

Of course, this was as long as they did not take up arms; had they done so, they would certainly have been met with reciprocal force, as they would then have taken on the title of "Muharib" (one at war with God and society).

Although this issue did not occur during the era of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), it did happen after him, specifically during the era of Amir al-Mu'minin (عليه السلام), who rose to battle against armed hypocrites.

Some have said that "jihad against the hypocrites" in the above verse refers to the implementation of legal punishments (Hudud)

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 319.

upon them, as most of those upon whom the Hudud were carried out were from the hypocrites. However, there is no evidence for this interpretation, nor for the claim that legal punishments were mostly applied to them in that era.

It is noteworthy that the above verse appears identically, without any addition or subtraction, in Surah al-Tawbah, verse 73.

Then, the discourse returns once more to the story of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives. To provide them with a vivid practical lesson, it mentions the condensed fate of two impious women who were in the houses of two great Prophets of God, and the fate of two believing and self-sacrificing women, one of whom was in the house of one of the most tyrannical men in history.

First, He says: "Allah presents an example for those who disbelieve: the wife of Noah and the wife of Lot" (ضَرَبَ اللَّهُ مَثَلًا لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا امْرَأَتَ نُوحٍ وَامْرَأَتَ لُوطٍ) [^1]. "They were under two of Our righteous servants but betrayed them" (كَانَتَا تَحْتَ عَبْدَيْنِ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا صَالِحَيْنِ فَخَانَتَاهُمَا).

"But they [the husbands] did not avail them against Allah at all, and it was said, 'Enter the Fire with those who enter'" (فَلَمْ يُغْنِيَا عَنْهُمَا مِنَ اللَّهِ شَيْئًا وَقِيلَ ادْخُلَا النَّارَ مَعَ الدَّاخِلِينَ).

In this way, He warns the two wives of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) who were involved in the incident of disclosing secrets and distressing him (صلى الله عليه وآله), that they should not imagine that being the wife of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) alone can prevent their punishment. Just as the connection of the wives of Noah and Lot was severed from the household of prophethood and revelation due to their betrayal, and they were overtaken by divine punishment.

Furthermore, it is a warning to all believers across all strata not to consider their ties with the Awliya' Allah (Friends of God) as a barrier against divine punishment in the event of sin and disobedience.

In some commentaries, it is mentioned that the name of Prophet Noah’s wife was "Wahlah" and the name of Prophet Lot’s wife was "Wal'ah" [^1], while others have written the opposite. [^2] In any case, these two women betrayed these two great Prophets. Of course, their betrayal was never a deviation from the path of chastity, for the wife of no Prophet has ever been tainted by unchastity. As explicitly stated in a hadith from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "No wife of a Prophet ever committed adultery." [^3] The betrayal of Lot’s wife was that she collaborated with the enemies of that Prophet (عليه السلام) and handed over the secrets of his house to the enemy, and Noah’s wife was the same.

Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Betrayal (Khiyanah) and hypocrisy (Nifaq) are one reality, except that 'betrayal' is used in contrast to 'covenant and trust,' while hypocrisy is used in religious matters."

[^1]: "Daraba" here takes two objects: "Imra'ata Nuhin" is the first object which has been placed later, and "Mathalan" is the second object. It is also possible that "Daraba" has one object, which is "Mathalan," and "Imra'ata Nuhin" is its substitute (Al-Bayan fi Gharib I'rab al-Qur'an, Vol. 2, p. 449). [^1]: Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 6680. [^2]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 28, p. 142. [^3]: Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 245.

The suitability of this story with the incident of disclosing the secrets of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) house also necessitates that this be the meaning of betrayal.

In any case, the above verse cuts off the false hopes of individuals who imagine that merely being associated with a great person like the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) can be the cause of their salvation (even if they are tainted in action), so that no one considers themselves immune in this regard. Thus, at the end of the verse, it says: "It will be said to them: Enter the Fire along with the other dwellers of Hell," meaning there is no distinction between you and others in this regard!

Then, He mentions two examples for the believers, saying: "And Allah presents an example for those who believe: the wife of Pharaoh, when she said, 'My Lord! Build for me near You a house in Paradise and save me from Pharaoh and his deeds and save me from the wrongdoing people'" (وَضَرَبَ اللَّهُ مَثَلًا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا امْرَأَتَ فِرْعَوْنَ إِذْ قَالَتْ رَبِّ ابْنِ لِي عِنْدَكَ بَيْتًا فِي الْجَنَّةِ وَنَجِّنِي مِنْ فِرْعَوْنَ وَعَمَلِهِ وَنَجِّنِي مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ).

It is well known that the name of Pharaoh’s wife was "Asiya" and her father’s name was " مزاحم" (Muzahim). It is said that when she witnessed the miracle of Moses (عليه السلام) against the sorcerers, the depths of her heart were illuminated by the light of faith, and from that moment she believed in Moses. She constantly kept her faith hidden, but faith and love for God are not things that can be concealed forever. When Pharaoh became aware of her faith, he forbade her many times and insisted that she let go of the religion of Moses (عليه السلام) and abandon his God, but this steadfast woman never surrendered to Pharaoh’s demand.

Finally, Pharaoh ordered her hands and feet to be fastened with nails, placed under the scorching sun, and a massive stone to be cast upon her chest. As she was spending the final moments of her life, her prayer was: "My Lord! Build for me a house in Paradise near Yourself, and save me from Pharaoh and his deeds, and save me from the wrongdoing people!" Allah answered the prayer of this pure, self-sacrificing, believing woman and placed her alongside the best women of the world like Maryam, as she is placed in the same rank in these verses.

In a narration from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read:

"The most excellent women of the people of Paradise are four: Khadijah daughter of Khuwaylid, Fatimah daughter of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله), Maryam daughter of 'Imran, and Asiya daughter of Muzahim, the wife of Pharaoh." [^1]

Interestingly, with these words, Pharaoh’s wife belittles Pharaoh’s grand palace and considers it as nothing compared to a house near the mercy of God. In this way, she answers those who advised her not to lose all the eye-catching possibilities available to her through "being the Queen of Egypt" by believing in a shepherd like Moses!

And with the phrase "وَنَجِّنِي مِنْ فِرْعَوْنَ وَعَمَلِهِ" (save me from Pharaoh and his deeds), she declares her disavowal of both Pharaoh himself and his oppressions and crimes.

And with the phrase "وَنَجِّنِي مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ" (and save me from the wrongdoing people), she reveals her lack of conformity with the polluted environment and her alienation from their crimes. How calculated are these three phrases expressed by this knowledgeable and self-sacrificing woman in the final moments of her life—phrases that can be inspiring for all believing men and women of the world. These phrases take away vain excuses from all those who consider the pressure of the environment or a spouse as a justification for abandoning obedience to God and piety.

Certainly, there was no glitter, glory, or majesty greater than the Pharaonic establishment, just as there was no pressure or torture beyond the tortures of the criminal Pharaoh. Yet, neither that glitter nor this pressure and torture brought that believing woman to her knees; she continued her path toward the pleasure of God until she sacrificed her life for the True Beloved.

It is noteworthy that she requests Allah to build her a house in Paradise and near Himself; being in Paradise is the physical aspect, and being near God is the spiritual aspect, and she combined both in one short phrase.

Then, He refers to the second distinguished woman who is considered a model for the believers, saying: And also "Allah has presented an example" of "Maryam, the daughter of 'Imran, who guarded her chastity" (وَمَرْيَمَ ابْنَتَ عِمْرَانَ الَّتِي أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَهَا) [^1].

[^1]: Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 246. [^1]: Regarding what is meant by the expression "Farj," we had a detailed explanation in Volume 13 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 494 (under verse 91 of Surah al-Anbiya').

"So We breathed into it [her garment] of Our spirit" (فَنَفَخْنَا فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِنَا).

And by the command of God, without having a husband, she brought forth a child who became an Ulul-'Azm (Arch-Prophet) of the Lord.

Then He adds: "And she believed in the words of her Lord and His scriptures" (وَصَدَّقَتْ بِكَلِمَاتِ رَبِّهَا وَكُتُبِهِ).

"And she was of the obedient" (وَكَانَتْ مِنَ الْقَانِتِينَ).

In terms of faith, she was at the highest level, believing in all heavenly books and divine commands; and in terms of action, she was constantly obedient to divine orders—a servant with her soul and heart in her hands, her eyes on the command, and her ears listening for the order.

The difference between "Words" (Kalimat) and "Books" (Kutub) might be that "Books" refers to all the heavenly scriptures revealed to the Prophets, while "Words" refers to revelations that did not take the form of a heavenly book.

Maryam was so believing in these words and books that the Quran refers to her as "Siddiqah" (The Truthful/The Confirmer) in verse 75 of Surah al-Ma'idah.

In various verses of the Quran, much is seen regarding the personality of this believing woman and her high status, a significant part of which is in the very Surah named after her (some have also described Siddiqah as meaning very truthful).

In any case, with these expressions, the Quran clears Maryam’s name from the improper words that a group of polluted and criminal Jews said about her, questioning her character and even her chastity, and it strikes a firm blow to the mouths of the slanderers.

The expression "فَنَفَخْنَا فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِنَا" (We breathed into it of Our spirit), as we have pointed out before, refers to a magnificent and exalted spirit. In other words, the attribution of "Spirit" to "Allah" is an "honorific attribution" (Idafah Tashrifiyyah) used to express the greatness of something, like attributing "House" to "God" in the expression "Bayt Allah" (House of Allah); otherwise, Allah has neither a spirit nor a house.

Strangely, some commentators have narrated a hadith regarding distinguished and high-ranking women near God, in which they have "counted Aisha as the most superior of them!" It would have been better if they had not mentioned this hadith in the commentary of Surah al-Tahrim, for this Surah loudly gives a message contrary to this fabricated hadith. As we saw, many Sunni commentators and historians have explicitly stated that the two women whom the verses of this Surah severely reproach and who became the cause of the anger of God and His Messenger were "Hafsa" and "Aisha," and this meaning is also explicitly stated in Sahih Bukhari. [^1] We ask all those who think freely to review the verses of this Surah once more and then clarify the value of such hadiths.

O Allah! Protect us from groundless and prejudiced loves and hates, and make us such that we are surrendered and humble with our entire being before the verses of Your Glorious Quran.

[^1]: Sahih Bukhari, Vol. 6, p. 195.

O Lord! May that day never come when Your great Prophet is displeased with our deeds and dissatisfied with our way of life.

O God! Grant us such steadfastness that the pressures and tortures of the Pharaohs of the time throughout the world have the least effect on our spirit and faith.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah al-Tahrim 7 Shawwal 1406
